title: In A Balcony
content:
В В В В First part

В В В В Constance and Norbert

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Now.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Not now.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Give me them again, those hands
В В В В Put them upon my forehead, how it throbs!
В В В В Press them before my eyes, the fire comes through.
В В В В You cruellest, you dearest in the world,
В В В В Let me! the Queen must grant whateпїЅer I ask
В В В В How can I gain you and not ask the Queen?
В В В В There she stays waiting for me, here stand you.
В В В В Some time or other this was to be asked,
В В В В Now is the one time what I ask, I gain
В В В В Let me ask now, Love!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Do, and ruin us.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Let it be now, Love! All my soul breaks forth.
В В В В How I do love you! give my love its way!
В В В В A man can have but one life and one death,
В В В В One heaven, one hell. Let me fulfil my fate
В В В В Grant me my heaven now. Let me know you mine,
В В В В Prove you mine, write my name upon your brow,
В В В В Hold you and have you, and then die away
В В В В If God please, with completion in my soul.

В В В В Constance

В В В В I am not yours then? how content this man?
В В В В I am not his, who change into himself,
В В В В Have passed into his heart and beat its beats,
В В В В Who give my hands to him, my eyes, my hair,
В В В В Give all that was of me away to him
В В В В So well, that now, my spirit turned his own,
В В В В Takes part with him against the woman here,
В В В В Bids him not stumble at so mere a straw
В В В В As caring that the world be cognisant
В В В В How he loves her and how she worships him .
В В В В You have this woman, not as yet that world.
В В В В Go on, I bid, nor stop to care for me
В В В В By saving what I cease to care about,
В В В В The courtly name and pride of circumstance
В В В В The name youпїЅll pick up and be cumbered with
В В В В Just for the poor paradeпїЅs sake, nothing more;
В В В В Just that the world may slip from under you
В В В В Just that the world may cry пїЅSo much for him
В В В В The man predestined to the heap of crowns!
В В В В There goes his chance of winning one, at least.пїЅ

В В В В Norbert

В В В В The world!

В В В В Constance

В В В В You love it. Love me quite as well,
В В В В And see if I shall pray for this in vain!
В В В В Why must you ponder what it knows or thinks?

В В В В Norbert

В В В В You pray for what, in vain?

В В В В Constance

В В В В Oh my heartпїЅs heart,
В В В В How I do love you, Norbert! that is right!
В В В В But listen, or I take my hands away.
В В В В You say, пїЅlet it be nowпїЅ you would go now
В В В В And tell the Queen, perhaps six steps from us,
В В В В You love me so you do, thank God!

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Thank God!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Yes, Norbert, but you fain would tell your love,
В В В В And, what succeeds the telling, ask of her
В В В В My hand. Now take this rose and look at it,
В В В В Listening to me. You are the minister,
В В В В The QueenпїЅs first favourite, nor without a cause.
В В В В To-night completes your wonderful yearпїЅs-work
В В В В (This palace-feast is held to celebrate)
В В В В Made memorable by her lifeпїЅs success,
В В В В That junction of two crowns on her sole head
В В В В Her house had only dreamed of anciently.
В В В В That this mere dream is grown a stable truth
В В В В To-nightпїЅs feast makes authentic. Whose the praise?
В В В В Whose genius, patience, energy, achieved
В В В В What turned the many heads and broke the hearts?
В В В В You are the fate your minuteпїЅs in the heaven.
В В В В Next comes the QueenпїЅs turn. Name your own reward!
В В В В With leave to clench the past, chain the to-come,
В В В В Put out an arm and touch and take the sun
В В В В And fix it ever full-faced on your earth,
В В В В Possess yourself supremely of her life,
В В В В You choose the single thing she will not grant
В В В В The very declaration of which choice
В В В В Will turn the scale and neutralise your work.
В В В В At best she will forgive you, if she can.
В В В В You think IпїЅll let you choose her cousinпїЅs hand?

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Wait. First, do you retain your old belief
В В В В The Queen is generous, nay, is just?

В В В В Constance

В В В В There, there!
В В В В So men make women love them, while they know
В В В В No more of womenпїЅs hearts than . . . look you here,
В В В В You that are just and generous beside,
В В В В Make it your own case. For example now,
В В В В IпїЅll say I let you kiss me and hold my hands
В В В В Why? do you know why? IпїЅll instruct you, then
В В В В The kiss, because you have a name at court,
В В В В This hand and this, that you may shut in each
В В В В A jewel, if you please to pick up such.
В В В В ThatпїЅs horrible! Apply it to the Queen
В В В В Suppose, I am the Queen to whom you speak.
В В В В пїЅI was a nameless man: you needed me:
В В В В Why did I proffer you my aid? there stood
В В В В A certain pretty Cousin by your side.
В В В В Why did I make such common cause with you?
В В В В Access to her had not been easy else.
В В В В You give my labours here abundant praise
В В В В пїЅFaith, labour, while she overlooked, grew play.
В В В В How shall your gratitude discharge itself?
В В В В Give me her hand!пїЅ

В В В В Norbert

В В В В And still I urge the same.
В В В В Is the Queen just? just generous or no!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Yes, just. You love a rose no harm in that
В В В В But was it for the roseпїЅs sake or mine
В В В В You put it in your bosom? mine, you said
В В В В Then mine you still must say or else be false.
В В В В You told the Queen you served her for herself
В В В В If so, to serve her was to serve yourself
В В В В She thinks, for all your unbelieving face!
В В В В I know her. In the hall, six steps from us,
В В В В One sees the twenty pictures thereпїЅs a life
В В В В Better than life and yet no life at all;
В В В В Conceive her born in such a magic dome,
В В В В Pictures all round her! why, she sees the world
В В В В Can recognise its given things and facts,
В В В В The fight of giants or the feast of gods,
В В В В Sages in senate, beauties at the bath,
В В В В Chaces and battles, the whole earthпїЅs display,
В В В В Landscape and sea-piece, down to flowers and fruit
В В В В And who shall question that she knows them all
В В В В In better semblance than the things outside?
В В В В Yet bring into the silent gallery
В В В В Some live thing to contrast in breath and blood,
В В В В Some lion, with the painted lion there
В В В В You think sheпїЅll understand composedly?
В В В В  Say, пїЅthatпїЅs his fellow in the hunting-piece
В В В В Yonder, IпїЅve turned to praise a hundred times?пїЅ
В В В В Not so. Her knowledge of our actual earth,
В В В В Its hopes and fears, concerns and sympathies,
В В В В Must be too far, too mediate, too unreal.
В В В В The real exists for us outside, not her
В В В В How should it, with that life in these four walls,
В В В В That father and that mother, first to last
В В В В No father and no mother friends, a heap,
В В В В Lovers, no lack a husband in due time,
В В В В And everyone of them alike a lie!
В В В В Things painted by a Rubens out of nought
В В В В Into what kindness, friendship, love should be;
В В В В All better, all more grandiose than life,
В В В В Only no life; mere cloth and surface-paint
В В В В You feel while you admire. How should she feel?
В В В В And now that she has stood thus fifty years
В В В В The sole spectator in that gallery,
В В В В You think to bring this warm real struggling love
В В В В In to her of a sudden, and suppose
В В В В SheпїЅll keep her state untroubled? HereпїЅs the truth
В В В В SheпїЅll apprehend its value at a glance,
В В В В Prefer it to the pictured loyalty!
В В В В You only have to say пїЅso men are made,
В В В В For this they act, the thing has many names
В В В В But this the right one and now, Queen, be just!пїЅ
В В В В And life slips back you lose her at the word
В В В В You do not even for amends gain me.
В В В В He will not understand! oh, Norbert, Norbert,
В В В В Do you not understand?

В В В В Norbert

В В В В The QueenпїЅs the Queen,
В В В В I am myself no picture, but alive
В В В В In every nerve and every muscle, here
В В В В At the palace-window or in the peopleпїЅs street,
В В В В As she in the gallery where the pictures glow.
В В В В The good of life is precious to us both.
В В В В She cannot love what do I want with rule?
В В В В When first I saw your face a year ago
В В В В I knew my lifeпїЅs good my soul heard one voice
В В В В пїЅThe woman yonder, thereпїЅs no use of life
В В В В But just to obtain her! heap earthпїЅs woes in one
В В В В And bear them make a pile of all earthпїЅs joys
В В В В And spurn them, as they help or help not here;
В В В В Only, obtain her!пїЅ How was it to be?
В В В В I found she was the cousin of the Queen;
В В В В I must then serve the Queen to get to her
В В В В No other way. Suppose there had been one,
В В В В And I by saying prayers to some white star
В В В В With promise of my body and my soul
В В В В Might gain you, should I pray the star or no?
В В В В Instead, there was the Queen to serve! I served,
В В В В And did what other servants failed to do.
В В В В Neither she sought nor I declared my end.
В В В В Her good is hers, my recompense be mine,
В В В В And let me name you as that recompense.

В В В В She dreamed that such a thing could never be?
В В В В Let her wake now. She thinks there was some cause
В В В В The love of power, of fame, pure loyalty?
В В В В  Perhaps she fancies men wear out their lives
В В В В Chasing such shades. Then IпїЅve a fancy too.
В В В В I worked because I want you with my soul
В В В В I therefore ask your hand. Let it be now.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Had I not loved you from the very first,
В В В В Were I not yours, could we not steal out thus
В В В В So wickedly, so wildly, and so well,
В В В В You might be thus impatient. WhatпїЅs conceived
В В В В Of us without here, by the folks within?
В В В В Where are you now? immersed in cares of state
В В В В Where am I now? intent on festal robes
В В В В We two, embracing under deathпїЅs spread hand!
В В В В What was this thought for, what this scruple of yours
В В В В Which broke the council up, to bring about
В В В В One minuteпїЅs meeting in the corridor?
В В В В And then the sudden sleights, long secresies,
В В В В The plots inscrutable, deep telegraphs,
В В В В Long-planned chance-meetings, hazards of a look,
В В В В пїЅDoes she know? does she not know? saved or lost?пїЅ
В В В В A year of this compressionпїЅs ecstasy
В В В В All goes for nothing? you would give this up
В В В В For the old way, the open way, the worldпїЅs,
В В В В His way who beats, and his who sells his wife?
В В В В What tempts you? their notorious happiness,
В В В В That youпїЅre ashamed of ours? The best youпїЅll get
В В В В Will be, the Queen grants all that you require,
В В В В Concedes the cousin, and gets rid of you
В В В В And her at once, and gives us ample leave
В В В В To live as our five hundred happy friends.
В В В В The world will show us with officious hand
В В В В Our chamber-entry and stand sentinel,
В В В В When we so oft have stolen across her traps!
В В В В Get the worldпїЅs warrant, ring the falconпїЅs foot,
В В В В And make it duty to be bold and swift,
В В В В When long ago пїЅtwas nature. Have it so!
В В В В He never hawked by rights till flung from fist?
В В В В Oh, the manпїЅs thought! no womanпїЅs such a fool.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Yes, the manпїЅs thought and my thought, which is more
В В В В One made to love you, let the world take note.
В В В В Have I done worthy work? be loveпїЅs the praise,
В В В В Though hampered by restrictions, barred against
В В В В By set forms, blinded by forced secresies.
В В В В Set free my love, and see what love will do
В В В В Shown in my life what work will spring from that!
В В В В The world is used to have its business done
В В В В On other grounds, find great effects produced
В В В В For powerпїЅs sake, fameпїЅs sake, motives you have named.
В В В В So good. But let my low ground shame their high.
В В В В Truth is the strong thing. Let manпїЅs life be true!
В В В В And loveпїЅs the truth of mine. Time prove the rest
В В В В I choose to have you stamped all over me,
В В В В Your name upon my forehead and my breast,
В В В В You, from the swordпїЅs blade to the ribbonпїЅs edge,
В В В В That men may see, all over, you in me
В В В В That pale loves may die out of their pretence
В В В В In face of mine, shames thrown on love fall off
В В В В Permit this, Constance! Love has been so long
В В В В Subdued in me, eating me through and through,
В В В В That now itпїЅs all of me and must have way.
В В В В Think of my work, that chaos of intrigues,
В В В В Those hopes and fears, surprises and delays,
В В В В That long endeavour, earnest, patient, slow,
В В В В Trembling at last to its assured result
В В В В Then think of this revulsion. I resume
В В В В Life, after death, (it is no less than life
В В В В After such long unlovely labouring days)
В В В В And liberate to beauty lifeпїЅs great need
В В В В Of the beautiful, which, while it prompted work,
В В В В Supprest itself erewhile. This eveпїЅs the time
В В В В This eve intense with yon first trembling star
В В В В We seem to pant and reach; scarce ought between
В В В В The earth that rises and the heaven that bends
В В В В All nature self-abandoned every tree
В В В В Flung as it will, pursuing its own thoughts
В В В В And fixed so, every flower and every weed,
В В В В No pride, no shame, no victory, no defeat:
В В В В All under God, each measured by itself
В В В В These statues round us, each abrupt, distinct,
В В В В The strong in strength, the weak in weakness fixed,
В В В В The Muse for ever wedded to her lyre,
В В В В The Nymph to her fawn, the Silence to her rose,
В В В В And GodпїЅs approval on his universe!
В В В В Let us do so aspire to live as these
В В В В In harmony with truth, ourselves being true.
В В В В Take the first way, and let the second come,
В В В В My first is to possess myself of you;
В В В В The music sets the march-step forward then!
В В В В And thereпїЅs the Queen, I go to claim you of,
В В В В The world to witness, wonder and applaud.
В В В В Our flower of life breaks open. No delay!

В В В В Constance

В В В В And so shall we be ruined, both of us.
В В В В Norbert, I know her to the skin and bone
В В В В You do not know her, were not born to it,
В В В В To feel what she can see or cannot see.
В В В В Love, she is generous, ay, despite your Smile,
В В В В Generous as you are. For, in that thin frame,
В В В В Pain-twisted, punctured through and through with cares,
В В В В There lived a lavish soul until it starved
В В В В Debarred all healthy food. Look to the soul
В В В В Pity that, stoop to that, ere you begin
В В В В (The true manпїЅs way) on justice and your rights,
В В В В Exactions and acquittance of the past.
В В В В Begin so see what justice she will deal!
В В В В We women hate a debt as men a gift.
В В В В Suppose her some poor keeper of a school
В В В В Whose business is to sit throпїЅ summer-months
В В В В And dole out childrenпїЅs leave to go and play,
В В В В Herself superior to such lightness she
В В В В In the arm-chairпїЅs state and pпїЅdagogic pomp,
В В В В To the life, the laughter, sun and youth outside
В В В В We wonder such an one looks black on us?
В В В В I do not bid you wake her tenderness,
В В В В That were vain truly, none is left to wake,
В В В В But, let her think her justice is engaged
В В В В To take the shape of tenderness, and mark
В В В В If sheпїЅll not coldly do its warmest deed!
В В В В Does she love me, I ask you? not a whit.
В В В В Yet, thinking that her justice was engaged
В В В В To help a kinswoman, she took me up,
В В В В Did more on that bare ground than other loves
В В В В Would do on greater argument. For me,
В В В В I have no equivalent of that cold kind
В В В В To pay her with; my love alone to give
В В В В If I give anything. I give her love.
В В В В I feel I ought to help her, and I will.
В В В В So for her sake, as yours, I tell you twice
В В В В That women hate a debt as men a gift.
В В В В If I were you, I could obtain this grace,
В В В В Would lay the whole I did to loveпїЅs account,
В В В В Nor yet be very false as courtiers go,
В В В В Declare that my success was recompense;
В В В В It would be so, in fact: what were it else?
В В В В And then, once loosed her generosity
В В В В As you will mark it, then, were I but you
В В В В To turn it, let it seem to move itself,
В В В В And make it give the thing I really take,
В В В В Accepting so, in the poor cousinпїЅs hand,
В В В В All value as the next thing to the queen,
В В В В Since none loves her directly, none dares that!
В В В В A shadow of a thing, a nameпїЅs mere echo
В В В В Suffices those who miss the name and thing;
В В В В You pick up just a ribbon she has worn
В В В В To keep in proof how near her breath you came.
В В В В Say IпїЅm so near I seem a piece of her,
В В В В Ask for me that way, (oh, you understand)
В В В В And find the same gift yielded with a grace,
В В В В Which if you make the least show to extort
В В В В YouпїЅll see! and when you have ruined both of us,
В В В В Dis[s]ertate on the QueenпїЅs ingratitude!

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Then, if I turn it that way, you consent?
В В В В пїЅTis not my way; I have more hope in truth.
В В В В Still if you wonпїЅt have truth, why, this indeed,
В В В В Is scarcely false, IпїЅll so express the sense.
В В В В Will you remain here?

В В В В Constance

В В В В O best heart of mine,
В В В В How I have loved you! then, you take my way?
В В В В Are mine as you have been her minister,
В В В В Work out my thought, give it effect for me,
В В В В Paint plain my poor conceit and make it serve?
В В В В I owe that withered woman everything,
В В В В Life, fortune, you, remember! Take my part,
В В В В Help me to pay her! Stand upon your rights?
В В В В You, with my rose, my hands, my heart on you?
В В В В Your rights are mine, you have no rights but mine.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Remain here. How you know me!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Ah, but still--

В В В В [He breaks from her: she remains.
В В В В Dance music from within.



В В В В SECOND PART

В В В В Enter the Queen

В В В В Queen

В В В В Constance! She is here as he said. Speak! quick!
В В В В Is it so? is it true, or false? One word!

В В В В Constance

В В В В True.
В В В В Queen

В В В В Mercifullest Mother, thanks to thee!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Madam
В В В В Queen

В В В В I love you, Constance, from my soul.
В В В В Now say once more, with any words you will,
В В В В пїЅTis true, all true, as true as that I speak,

В В В В Constance

В В В В Why should you doubt it?

В В В В Queen

В В В В Ah, why doubt? why doubt?
В В В В Dear, make me see it. Do you see it so?
В В В В None see themselves, another sees them best.
В В В В You say пїЅwhy doubt it?пїЅ you see him and me.
В В В В It is because the Mother has such grace

В В В В WhateпїЅer we yearn for would be granted us;
В В В В Howbeit we let our whims prescribe despair,
В В В В Our very fancies thwart and cramp our will,

В В В В Constance, I had abjured the hope of love
В В В В And of being loved, as truly as yon palm
В В В В The hope of seeing Egypt from that turf.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Heaven!
В В В В Queen

В В В В But it was so, Constance, it was so.
В В В В Men say, or do men say it? fancies say,
В В В В пїЅStop here, your life is set, you are grown old;
В В В В Too late, no love for you, too late for love,
В В В В Leave love to girls. Be queen, let Constance love!пїЅ
В В В В One takes the hint, half meets it like a child,
В В В В Ashamed at any feelings that oppose.
В В В В пїЅOh, love, true, never think of love again
В В В В I am a queen, I rule, not love, indeed.пїЅ
В В В В So it goes on; so a face grows like this,
В В В В Hair like this hair, poor arms as lean as these,
В В В В Till, nay, it does not end so, I thank God! 433

В В В В Constance

В В В В I cannot understand--
В В В В Queen

В В В В The happier you!
В В В В Constance, I know not how it is with men.
В В В В For women, (I am a woman now like you)
В В В В There is no good of life but love, but love!
В В В В What else looks good, is some shade flung from love,
В В В В Love gilds it, gives it worth. Be warned by me,
В В В В Never you cheat yourself one instant. Love,
В В В В Give love, ask only love, and leave the rest!
В В В В O Constance, how I love you!

В В В В Constance

В В В В I love you.
В В В В Queen

В В В В I do believe that all is come through you.
В В В В I took you to my heart to keep it warm
В В В В When the last chance of love seemed dead in me;
В В В В I thought your fresh youth warmed my withered heart.
В В В В Oh, I am very old now, am I not?
В В В В Not so! it is true and it shall be true!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Queen

В В В В And say пїЅsheпїЅs old, sheпїЅs grown unlovely quite
В В В В Who neпїЅer was beauteous! men want beauty still.пїЅ
В В В В Well, so I feared, the curse! so I felt sure.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Be calm. And now you feel not sure, you say?

В В В В Queen

В В В В Constance, he came, the coming was not strange,
В В В В Do not I stand and see men come and go?
В В В В I turned a half look from my pedestal
В В В В Where I grow marble, пїЅone young man the more!
В В В В He will love some one, that is nought to me,
В В В В What would he with my marble stateliness?пїЅ
В В В В Yet this seemed somewhat worse than heretofore;
В В В В The man more gracious, youthful, like a god,
В В В В And I still older, with less flesh to change,
В В В В We two those dear extremes that long to touch.
В В В В It seemed still harder when he first began
В В В В Absorbed to labour at the state-affairs
В В В В The old way for the old end, interest.
В В В В Oh, to live with a thousand beating hearts
В В В В Around you, swift eyes, serviceable hands,
В В В В Professing theyпїЅve no care but for your cause,
В В В В Thought but to help you, love but for yourself,
В В В В And you the marble statue all the time
В В В В They praise and point at as preferred to life,
В В В В Yet leave for the first breathing womanпїЅs cheek,
В В В В First dancerпїЅs, gypsyпїЅs, or street baladineпїЅs!
В В В В Why, how I have ground my teeth to hear menпїЅs speech
В В В В Stifled for fear it should alarm my ear,
В В В В Their gait subdued lest step should startle me,
В В В В Their eyes declined, such queendom to respect,
В В В В Their hands alert, such treasure to preserve,
В В В В While not a man of these broke rank and spoke,
В В В В Or wrote me a vulgar letter all of love,
В В В В Or caught my hand and pressed it like a hand.
В В В В There have been moments, if the sentinel
В В В В Lowering his halbert to salute the queen,
В В В В Had flung it brutally and clasped my knees,
В В В В I would have stooped and kissed him, with my soul.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Who could have comprehended!

В В В В Queen

В В В В Ay, who, who?
В В В В Why, no one, Constance, but this one who did.
В В В В Not they, not you, not I. Even now perhaps
В В В В it comes too late, would you but tell the truth.

В В В В Constance

В В В В I wait to tell it.

В В В В Queen

В В В В Well, you see, he came,
В В В В Outfaced the others, did a work this year
В В В В Exceeds in value all was ever done
В В В В You know, it is not I who say it, all
В В В В Say it, And so (a second pang and worse)
В В В В I grew aware not only of what he did,
В В В В But why so wondrously. Oh, never work
В В В В Like his was done for workпїЅs ignoble sake,
В В В В It must have finer aims to spur it on!
В В В В I felt, I saw he loved, loved somebody.

В В В В I did believe this while пїЅtwas you he loved.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Me, madam?

В В В В Queen

В В В В It did seem to me your face
В В В В Met him, whereпїЅer he looked: and whom but you
В В В В Was such a man to love? it seemed to me
В В В В You saw he loved you, and approved the love,
В В В В And that you both were in intelligence.
В В В В You could not loiter in the garden, step
В В В В Into this balcony, but I straight was stung
В В В В And forced to understand. It seemed so true,
В В В В So right, so beautiful, so like you both
В В В В That all this work should have been done by him,
В В В В Not for the vulgar hope of recompense,

В В В В And I (O Constance, you shall love me now)
В В В В I thought, surmounting all the bitterness,
В В В В пїЅAnd he shall have it. I will make her blest,
В В В В My flower of youth, my womanпїЅs self that was,
В В В В My happiest womanпїЅs self that might have been!
В В В В These two shall have their joy and leave me here.пїЅ
В В В В Yes, yes,

В В В В Constance

В В В В Thanks!
В В В В Queen

В В В В A mere calm statement of his just desire
В В В В In payment of his labour. When, O Heaven,
В В В В How can I tell you? cloud was on my eyes

В В В В He loved me, from the first step to the last,
В В В В Loved me!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Queen

В В В В No, no,
В В В В No mistake! Ha, there shall be no mistake!
В В В В He had not dared to hint the love he felt,
В В В В You were my reflex, how I understood!
В В В В He said you were the ribbon I had worn,
В В В В He kissed my hand, he looked into my eyes,

В В В В Love is begun, this much is come to pass,
В В В В The rest is easy. Constance, I am yours,
В В В В I will learn, I will place my life on you,
В В В В But teach me how to keep what I have won.

В В В В But joy ere now has brought hair brown again,
В В В В And joy will bring the cheekпїЅs red back, I feel.
В В В В I could sing once too; that was in my youth.
В В В В Still, when men paint me, they declare me . . . yes,
В В В В Beautiful,for the last French Painter did!
В В В В I know they flatter somewhat;, you are frank,
В В В В I trust you. How I loved you from the first!
В В В В Some queens would hardly seek a cousin out
В В В В And set her by their side to take the eye
В В В В I must have felt that good would come from you.
В В В В I am not generous, like him, like you!
В В В В But he is not your lover after all,
В В В В It was not you he looked at. Saw you him?
В В В В You have not been mistaking words or looks?
В В В В He said you were the reflex of myself,
В В В В And yet he is not such a paragon
В В В В To you, to younger women who may choose
В В В В Among a thousand Norberts. Speak the truth!
В В В В You know you never named his name to me,
В В В В You know, I cannot give him up,all God,
В В В В Not up now, even to you!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Then calm yourself.

В В В В Queen

В В В В See, I am old, look here, you happy girl,
В В В В I will not play the fool, deceive myself;
В В В В пїЅTis all gone, put your cheek beside my cheek.
В В В В Ah, what a contrast does the moon behold!
В В В В But then I set my life upon one chance,
В В В В The last chance and the best, am I not left,
В В В В My soul, myself? All women love great men
В В В В If young or old, it is in all the tales,
В В В В Young beauties love old poets who can love,

В В В В Who was a queen and loved a poet once
В В В В Humpbacked, a dwarf? all, women can do that

В В В В They love so many women in their youth,

В В В В They like soul,well then, they like phantasy,
В В В В Novelty even. Let us confess the truth
В В В В Horrible though it be, that prejudice,
В В В В Prescription . . . Curses! they will love a queen.
В В В В They will, they do. And will not, does not he?

В В В В Constance

В В В В How can he? You are wedded пїЅtis a name

В В В В Queen

В В В В What did I say of smooth-cheeked youth and grace
В В В В See all it does or could do I so, youth loves!
В В В В Oh, tell him, Constance, you could never do
В В В В What I will you, it was not born in! I
В В В В Will drive these difficulties far and fast

В В В В IпїЅll use my light too, gloriously retrieve
В В В В My youth from its enforced calamity,

В В В В Constance

В В В В You will do dare do Pause on what you say!

В В В В Queen

В В В В Hear her! I thank you, Sweet, for that surprise.
В В В В You have the fair face: for the soul, see mine!
В В В В I have the strong soul: let me teach you, here.
В В В В I think I have borne enough and long enough,
В В В В And patiently enough, the world remarks,
В В В В To have my own way now, unblamed by all.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Queen

В В В В You, Constance, learn of me; do you, like me.
В В В В You are young, beautiful: my own, best girl,
В В В В You will have many lovers, and love one

В В В В Constance

В В В В He shall.

В В В В Queen

В В В В This he does, what should this amount to, pray?

В В В В Your love (according to your beautyпїЅs worth)
В В В В For you shall have some noble love, all gold

В В В В Many remain God has been very good.

В В В В PART THIRD

В В В В Norbert enters

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Well! we have but one minute and one word

В В В В Constance
В В В В I am yours, Norbert!

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Yes, mine.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Not till now!
В В В В You were mine. Now I give myself to you.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Constance!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Your own! I know the thriftier way
В В В В Of giving haply, пїЅtis the wiser way.
В В В В Meaning to give a treasure, I might dole
В В В В Coin after coin out (each, as that were all,
В В В В With a new largess still at each despair)
В В В В And force you keep in sight the deed, reserve
В В В В Exhaustless till the end my part and yours,
В В В В My giving and your taking, both our joys
В В В В Dying together. Is it the wiser way?
В В В В I choose the simpler; I give all at once.
В В В В Know what you have to trust to, trade upon.
В В В В Use it, abuse it, anything but say
В В В В Hereafter, пїЅHad I known she loved me so,
В В В В And what my means, I might have thriven with it.пїЅ
В В В В This is your means. I give you all myself.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В I take you and thank God.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Look on through years!
В В В В We cannot kiss a second day like this,
В В В В Else were this earth, no earth.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В With this dayпїЅs heat
В В В В We shall go on through years of cold.

В В В В Constance

В В В В В В В В  So best.
В В В В I try to see those years I think I see.
В В В В You walk quick and new warmth comes; you look back
В В В В And lay all to the first glow not sit down
В В В В For ever brooding on a day like this
В В В В While seeing the embers whiten and love die.
В В В В Yes, love lives best in its effect; and mine,
В В В В Full in its own life, yearns to live in yours.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Just so. I take and know you all at once.
В В В В Your soul is disengaged so easily,
В В В В Your face is there, I know you; give me time,
В В В В Let me be proud and think you shall know me.
В В В В My soul is slower: in a life I roll
В В В В The minute out in which you condense yours
В В В В The whole slow circle round you I must move,
В В В В To be just you. I look to a long life
В В В В To decompose this minute, prove its worth.
В В В В пїЅTis the sparksпїЅ long succession one by one
В В В В Shall show you in the end what fire was crammed
В В В В In that mere stone you struck: you could not know,
В В В В If it lay ever unproved in your sight,
В В В В As now my heart lies? your own warmth would hide
В В В В Its coldness, were it cold.

В В В В Constance

В В В В But how prove, how?

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Prove in my life, you ask?

В В В В Constance

В В В В Quick, Norbert how?

В В В В Norbert

В В В В ThatпїЅs easy told. I count life just a stuff
В В В В To try the soulпїЅs strength on, educe the man.
В В В В Who keeps one end in view makes all things serve.
В В В В As with the body he who hurls a lance
В В В В Or heaps up stone on stone, shows strength alike,
В В В В So I will seize and use all means to prove
В В В В And show this soul of mine you crown as yours,
В В В В And justify us both.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Could you write books,
В В В В Paint pictures! one sits down in poverty
В В В В And writes or paints, with pity for the rich.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В And loves oneпїЅs painting and oneпїЅs writing too,
В В В В And not oneпїЅs mistress! All is best, believe,
В В В В And we best as no other than we are.
В В В В We live, and they experiment on life
В В В В Those poets, painters, all who stand aloof
В В В В To overlook the farther. Let us be
В В В В The thing they look at! I might take that face
В В В В And write of it and paint it to what end?
В В В В For whom? what pale dictatress in the air
В В В В Feeds, smiling sadly, her fine ghost-like form

В В В В She makes despised for ever? You are mine,
В В В В Made for me, not for others in the world,
В В В В Nor yet for that which I should call my art,
В В В В That cold calm power to see how fair you look.
В В В В I come to you I leave you not, to write
В В В В Or paint. You are, I am. Let Rubens there
В В В В Paint us.

В В В В Constance
В В В В So best!

В В В В Norbert

В В В В I understand your soul.
В В В В You live, and rightly sympathise with life,
В В В В With action, power, success: this way is straight.
В В В В And days were short beside, to let me change
В В В В The craft my childhood learnt; my craft shall serve.
В В В В Men set me here to subjugate, enclose,
В В В В Manure their barren lives and force the fruit
В В В В First for themselves, and afterward for me
В В В В In the due tithe; the task of some one man,
В В В В By ways of work appointed by themselves.
В В В В I am not bid create, they see no star
В В В В Transfiguring my brow to warrant that
В В В В But bind in one and carry out their wills.
В В В В So I began: to-night sees how I end.
В В В В What if it see, too, my first outbreak here
В В В В Amid the warmth, surprise and sympathy,
В В В В The instincts of the heart that teach the head?
В В В В What if the people have discerned in me
В В В В The dawn of the next nature, the new man
В В В В Whose will they venture in the place of theirs,
В В В В And whom they trust to find them out new ways
В В В В To the new heights which yet he only sees?
В В В В I felt it when you kissed me. See this Queen,
В В В В This people in our phrase, this mass of men
В В В В See how the mass lies passive to my hand
В В В В And how my hand is plastic, and you by
В В В В To make the muscles iron! Oh, an end
В В В В Shall crown this issue as this crowns the first.
В В В В My will be on this people! then, the strain,
В В В В The grappling of the potter with his clay,
В В В В The long uncertain struggle, the success
В В В В In that uprising of the spirit-work,
В В В В The vase shaped to the curl of the godпїЅs lip,
В В В В While rounded fair for lower men to see
В В В В The Graces in a dance they recognise
В В В В With turbulent applause and laughs of heart!
В В В В So triumph ever shall renew itself;
В В В В Ever to end in efforts higher yet,
В В В В Ever begun

В В В В Constance

В В В В I ever helping?

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Thus!

В В В В [As he embraces her, enter the Queen.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Hist, madam so I have performed my part.
В В В В You see your gratitudeпїЅs true decency,
В В В В Norbert? a little slow in seeing it!
В В В В Begun to end the sooner. WhatпїЅs a kiss?

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Constance!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Why, must I teach it you again?
В В В В You want a witness to your dullness, sir?
В В В В What was I saying these ten minutes long?
В В В В Then I repeat when some young handsome man
В В В В Like you has acted out a part like yours,
В В В В Is pleased to fall in love with one beyond,
В В В В So very far beyond him, as he says
В В В В So hopelessly in love, that but to speak
В В В В Would prove him mad, he thinks judiciously,
В В В В And makes some insignificant good soul
В В В В Like me, his friend, adviser, confidant
В В В В And very stalking-horse to cover him
В В В В In following after what he dares not face
В В В В When his endпїЅs gained (sir, do you understand?)
В В В В When she, he dares not face, has loved him first,
В В В В  May I not say so, madam? tops his hope,
В В В В And overpasses so his wildest dream,
В В В В With glad consent of all, and most of her
В В В В The confidant who brought the same about
В В В В Why, in the moment when such joy explodes,
В В В В I do say that the merest gentleman
В В В В Will not start rudely from the stalking-horse,
В В В В Dismiss it with a пїЅThere, enough of you!пїЅ
В В В В Forget it, show his back unmannerly;
В В В В But like a liberal heart will rather turn
В В В В And say, пїЅA tingling time of hope was ours
В В В В Betwixt the fears and falterings we two lived
В В В В A chanceful time in waiting for the prize.
В В В В The confidant, the Constance, served not ill;
В В В В And though I shall forget her in due time,
В В В В Her use being answered now, as reason bids,
В В В В Nay as herself bids from her heart of hearts,
В В В В Still, she has rights, the first thanks go to her,
В В В В The first good praise goes to the prosperous tool,
В В В В And the first which is the last thankful kiss.пїЅ

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Constance? it is a dream ah see you smile!

В В В В Constance

В В В В So, now his part being properly performed,
В В В В Madam, I turn to you and finish mine
В В В В As duly I do justice in my turn.
В В В В Yes, madam, he has loved you long and well
В В В В He could not hope to tell you so пїЅtwas I
В В В В Who served to prove your soul accessible.
В В В В I led his thoughts on, drew them to their place,
В В В В When oft they had wandered out into despair,
В В В В And kept love constant toward its natural aim.
В В В В Enough my part is played; you stoop half-way
В В В В And meet us royally and spare our fears
В В В В пїЅTis like yourself he thanks you, so do I.
В В В В Take him with my full heart! my work is praised
В В В В By what comes of it. Be you happy, both!
В В В В Yourself the only one on earth who can
В В В В Do all for him, much more than a mere heart
В В В В Which though warm is not useful in its warmth
В В В В As the silk vesture of a queen! fold that
В В В В Around him gently, tenderly. For him
В В В В For him, he knows his own part.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Have you done?
В В В В I take the jest at last. Should I speak now?
В В В В Was yours the wager, Constance, foolish child,
В В В В Or did you but accept it? Well at least,
В В В В You lose by it.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Now madam, пїЅtis your turn.
В В В В Restrain him still from speech a little more
В В В В And make him happier and more confident
В В В В Pity him, madam, he is timid yet.
В В В В Mark, Norbert! do not shrink now! Here I yield
В В В В My whole right in you to the Queen, observe!
В В В В With her go put in practice the great schemes
В В В В You teem with, follow the career else closed
В В В В Be all you cannot be except by her!
В В В В Behold her. Madam, say for pityпїЅs sake
В В В В Anything frankly say you love him. Else
В В В В HeпїЅll not believe it: thereпїЅs more earnest in
В В В В His fear than you conceive I know the man.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В I know the woman somewhat, and confess
В В В В I thought she had jested better she begins
В В В В To overcharge her part. I gravely wait
В В В В Your pleasure, madam: where is my reward?

В В В В Queen

В В В В Norbert, this wild girl (whom I recognise
В В В В Scarce more than you do, in her fancy-fit,
В В В В Eccentric speech and variable mirth,
В В В В Not very wise perhaps and somewhat bold
В В В В Yet suitable, the whole nightпїЅs work being strange)
В В В В  May still be right: I may do well to speak
В В В В And make authentic what appears a dream
В В В В To even myself. For, what she says, is true
В В В В Yes, Norbert what you spoke but now of love,
В В В В Devotion, stirred no novel sense in me,
В В В В But justified a warmth felt long before.
В В В В Yes, from the first I loved you, I shall say,
В В В В Strange! but I do grow stronger, now пїЅtis said,
В В В В Your courage helps mine: you did well to speak
В В В В To-night, the night that crowns your twelvemonthsпїЅ toil
В В В В But still I had not waited to discern
В В В В Your heart so long, believe me! From the first
В В В В The source of so much zeal was almost plain,
В В В В In absence even of your own words just now
В В В В Which opened out the truth. пїЅTis very strange,
В В В В But takes a happy ending in your love
В В В В Which mine meets: be it so as you choose me,
В В В В So I choose you.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В And worthily you choose!
В В В В I will not be unworthy your esteem,
В В В В No, madam. I do love you; I will meet
В В В В Your nature, now I know it; this was well,
В В В В I see, you dare and you are justified:
В В В В But none had ventured such experiment,
В В В В Less versed than you in nobleness of heart,
В В В В Less confident of finding it in me.
В В В В I like that thus you test me ere you grant
В В В В The dearest, richest, beauteousest and best
В В В В Of women to my arms! пїЅTis like yourself!
В В В В So back again into my partпїЅs set words
В В В В Devotion to the uttermost is yours,
В В В В But no, you cannot, madam, even you,
В В В В Create in me the love our Constance does.
В В В В Or something truer to the tragic phrase
В В В В Not yon magnolia-bell superb with scent
В В В В Invites a certain insect thatпїЅs myself
В В В В But the small eye-flower nearer to the ground
В В В В I take this lady!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Stay not hers, the trap
В В В В Stay, Norbert that mistake were worst of all.
В В В В (He is too cunning, madam!) it was I,
В В В В I, Norbert, who . . .

В В В В Norbert

В В В В You, was it, Constance? Then,
В В В В But for the grace of this divinest hour
В В В В Which gives me you, I should not pardon here.
В В В В I am the QueenпїЅs: she only knows my brain
В В В В She may experiment therefore on my heart
В В В В And I instruct her too by the result;
В В В В But you, sweet, you who know me, who so long
В В В В Have told my heart-beats over, held my life
В В В В In those white hands of yours, it is not well!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Tush! I have said it, did I not say it all?
В В В В The life, for her the heart-beats, for her sake!

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Enough! my cheek grows red, I think. Your test
В В В В ThereпїЅs not the meanest woman in the world,
В В В В Not she I least could love in all the world,
В В В В Whom, did she love me, did love prove itself,
В В В В I dared insult as you insult me now.
В В В В Constance, I could say, if it must be said,
В В В В пїЅTake back the soul you offer I keep mineпїЅ
В В В В But пїЅTake the soul still quivering on your hand,
В В В В The soul so offered, which I cannot use,
В В В В And, please you, give it to some friend of mine,
В В В В For whatпїЅs the trifle he requites me with?пїЅ
В В В В I, tempt a woman, to amuse a man,
В В В В That two may mock her heart if it succumb?
В В В В No! fearing God and standing пїЅneath his heaven,
В В В В I would not dare insult a woman so,
В В В В Were she the meanest woman in the world,
В В В В And he, I cared to please, ten emperors!

В В В В Constance

В В В В Norbert!

В В В В Norbert

В В В В I love once as I live but once.
В В В В What case is this to think or talk about?
В В В В I love you. Would it mend the case at all
В В В В Should such a step as this kill love in me?
В В В В Your part were done: account to God for it.
В В В В But mine could murdered love get up again,
В В В В And kneel to whom you pleased to designate
В В В В And make you mirth? It is too horrible.
В В В В You did not know this, Constance? now you know
В В В В That body and soul have each one life, but one
В В В В And hereпїЅs my love, here, living, at your feet.

В В В В Constance

В В В В See the Queen! Norbert this one more last word
В В В В If thus you have taken jest for earnest thus
В В В В Loved me in earnest . . .

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Ah, no jest holds here!
В В В В Where is the laughter in which jests break up?
В В В В And what this horror that grows palpable?
В В В В Madam why grasp you thus the balcony?
В В В В Have I done ill? Have I not spoken the truth?
В В В В How could I other? Was it not your test,
В В В В To try me, and what my love for Constance meant?
В В В В Madam, your royal soul itself approves,
В В В В The first, that I should choose thus! so one takes
В В В В A beggar asks him what would buy his child,
В В В В And then approves the expected laugh of scorn
В В В В Returned as something noble from the rags.
В В В В Speak, Constance, IпїЅm the beggar! Ha, whatпїЅs this?
В В В В You two glare each at each like panthers now.
В В В В Constance the world fades; only you stand there!
В В В В You did not in to-nightпїЅs wild whirl of things
В В В В Sell me your soul of souls for any price?
В В В В No no пїЅtis easy to believe in you.
В В В В Was it your loveпїЅs mad trial to oпїЅertop
В В В В Mine by this vain self-sacrifice? well, still
В В В В Though I should curse, I love you. I am love
В В В В And cannot change! loveпїЅs self is at your feet.

В В В В [Queen goes out.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Feel my heart; let it die against your own.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Against my own! explain not; let this be.
В В В В This is lifeпїЅs height.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Yours! Yours! Yours!

В В В В Norbert

В В В В You and I
В В В В Why care by what meanders we are here
В В В В In the centre of the labyrinth? men have died
В В В В Trying to find this place out, which we have found.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Found, found!

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Sweet, never fear what she can do
В В В В We are past harm now.

В В В В Constance

В В В В On the breast of God.
В В В В I thought of men as if you were a man.
В В В В Tempting him with a crown! 452

В В В В Norbert

В В В В This must end here
В В В В It is too perfect!

В В В В Constance

В В В В ThereпїЅs the music stopped.
В В В В What measured heavy tread? it is one blaze
В В В В About me and within me.

В В В В Norbert

В В В В Oh, some death
В В В В Will run its sudden finger round this spark,
В В В В And sever us from the rest

В В В В Constance

В В В В And so do well.
В В В В Now the doors open

В В В В Norbert

В В В В пїЅTis the guard comes.

В В В В Constance

В В В В Kiss!
meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
